steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The study found that participants recruited via snowball sampling (i.e., through their own social networks) had a much higher retention rate than those recruited by strangers. The blank must explain why that difference exists — what does being recruited by someone you know do that a stranger cannot?

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘being recruited by someone with whom one is socially connected may impart a feeling of obligation to persist’: A social connection creates accountability — you don't want to let down someone you know — which explains why retention is higher among snowball-sampled participants. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘people with small social networks are less likely to be recruited via snowball sampling’: This is about who gets recruited, not about why recruits stay once enrolled. It does not address retention. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘snowball sampling improves retention more for rural participants than nonrural participants’: The study compared two rural counties only; no urban or nonrural group was studied, so this rural/nonrural comparison is unsupported. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘snowball sampling produces more nationally representative samples than other methods’: Representativeness was never measured or mentioned in the passage. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
